metadata.name: FEHB Retirement Eligibility Rules
metadata.description: Rules governing Federal Employees Health Benefits (FEHB) continuation into retirement, including the 5-year enrollment requirement, premium payment mechanics, Medicare coordination, and special situations.
notes: FEHB is one of the most valuable federal retirement benefits — no age-out, no medical underwriting.
Unlike private employer health insurance, FEHB coverage continues for life if eligibility is met.
The government continues to pay its share of FEHB premiums for retirees (same percentage as active employees).
FEHB does NOT automatically coordinate with Medicare — retirees must make active enrollment decisions.

five_year_rule.description: To continue FEHB into retirement, the annuitant must have been continuously enrolled in FEHB (or TRICARE/CHAMPVA equivalent in some cases) for the 5 years of service immediately preceding retirement

alternatives.rule: Must have been enrolled since the first opportunity to enroll

alternatives.condition: Break in FEHB enrollment during the 5 years
alternatives.rule: Breaks for any reason (including temporary waiver) may disqualify the retiree from continuing FEHB
exceptions: Break due to covered-by-TRICARE (military spouse or own TRICARE entitlement) may be counted as continuous enrollment
Break due to CHAMPVA coverage may be counted
Transfer between agencies with no enrollment gap

five_year_rule.critical_planning_note: This is the single most important FEHB eligibility rule. Employees who waive FEHB for any reason (e.g., to use a spouse's insurance) during the final 5 years risk losing FEHB in retirement permanently.

immediate_retirees.description: Employees who retire on an immediate annuity (FERS or CSRS) and meet the 5-year rule

disability_retirees.description: FERS or CSRS disability retirees who meet the 5-year rule

deferred_retirees.eligible: Generally no — deferred annuitants lose FEHB eligibility at separation
deferred_retirees.exception: If receiving an annuity based on a discontinued service retirement or early retirement, may retain eligibility
deferred_retirees.note: This is a significant gap — employees who separate before retirement age and defer their annuity typically cannot continue FEHB

survivor_annuitants.description: Spouses (or former spouses with court-ordered coverage) receiving a survivor annuity may continue FEHB if the deceased annuitant was enrolled at death
survivor_annuitants.requirement: The deceased annuitant must have been enrolled in FEHB (self + family or self + one) at the time of death

government_share.description: The government continues to pay its share of FEHB premiums for retirees
government_share.pct_of_total: The government pays the lesser of: 72% of the weighted average premium of all FEHB plans, or 75% of the specific plan's total premium
government_share.note: In practice, this means the government pays roughly 70-75% of the premium, same as for active employees

annuitant_share.description: The retiree's share is deducted directly from their monthly annuity payment
annuitant_share.note: Unlike active employees (who can use premium conversion for pre-tax deductions), retiree FEHB premiums are paid with after-tax dollars from the annuity. This is a meaningful tax difference.

premium_mechanics_in_retirement.no_age_increase: FEHB premiums are NOT age-rated. A 90-year-old retiree pays the same as a 30-year-old active employee for the same plan.
premium_mechanics_in_retirement.plan_changes: Retirees can change plans during FEHB Open Season (November–December each year), just like active employees

enrollment_at_65.description: When an FEHB-enrolled retiree becomes eligible for Medicare at age 65, they have choices to make

medicare_part_a.cost: Premium-free if 40+ quarters of Medicare-covered employment
medicare_part_a.note: Almost all federal retirees should enroll in Part A (free). FERS employees paid Medicare tax and qualify. Some long-career CSRS employees may not have 40 quarters.

medicare_part_b.note: Not required to keep FEHB. However, if the retiree wants FEHB to act as secondary insurance (Medicare pays first), Part B enrollment is beneficial.
medicare_part_b.penalty: If not enrolled at 65 and no special enrollment period, late enrollment penalty of 10% per 12-month delay applies to Part B premiums permanently.
medicare_part_b.special_enrollment: Federal retirees do NOT get a special enrollment period for Part B after retirement — the standard enrollment windows apply.

fehb_as_secondary.description: When a retiree has both FEHB and Medicare, Medicare typically pays first (primary) and FEHB pays second (secondary)
fehb_as_secondary.benefit: This combination often results in very low out-of-pocket costs, as FEHB covers most remaining expenses after Medicare
fehb_as_secondary.note: Some FEHB plans waive copays, deductibles, or coinsurance when Medicare is the primary payer

suspend_fehb.description: Retirees can suspend FEHB enrollment to enroll in Medicare Advantage or other coverage
suspend_fehb.reinstatement: Can reinstate FEHB within specified timeframes without losing eligibility
suspend_fehb.caution: Suspending FEHB has risks — reinstatement rules are strict and may limit plan choices

temporary_continuation_of_coverage.description: Former employees who do not meet the 5-year rule may be eligible for TCC — the federal equivalent of COBRA
temporary_continuation_of_coverage.cost: 102% of the total premium (employee share + government share + 2% administrative fee)
temporary_continuation_of_coverage.note: TCC is expensive because the separating employee pays the full premium. It is a bridge, not a long-term solution.

postal_service_note.description: Effective January 2025, the Postal Service Health Benefits (PSHB) Program was established for Postal Service employees and retirees
postal_service_note.note: Postal employees and retirees transitioned to PSHB plans. Non-postal federal employees and retirees remain in FEHB.
